planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring before new growth begins, or plant container-grown specimens in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Colchis barrenwort is one of the most drought-tolerant and vigorous of all epimediums and is the parent of the widely grown E. × versicolor hybrids — it performs admirably in the dry shade of competitive tree roots where most shade groundcovers fail.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in well-drained, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; Colchis barrenwort is a Caucasian (Georgian and Iranian) epimedium species recognized as one of the most vigorous, drought-tolerant, and adaptable of the garden epimediums — it forms a steadily spreading, dense, weed-suppressing mat of compound, heart-shaped, spiny-edged, leathery, semi-evergreen leaves that are bronzed on emergence in spring before maturing to glossy, dark green. In spring, airy sprays of small, clear-yellow, four-petaled, spurred flowers — quite different from the long-spurred flowers of E. grandiflorum — rise just above the foliage. The species is one parent of E. × versicolor 'Sulphureum', the most commonly grown yellow epimedium. Colchis barrenwort is exceptional in its ability to spread and provide effective weed suppression in the dry shade under mature deciduous and coniferous trees. The previous season's foliage persists as an attractive bronze groundcover through winter before being replaced by spring growth. Among the very best plants for dry, difficult shade in zone 4–8.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; fringe cups self-seeds freely in moist shade conditions and may naturalize into a loose colony — deadhead spent flower spikes to reduce self-seeding in formal garden settings or allow to naturalize in woodland garden situations.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; fringe cups is a Pacific Northwest and Western North American woodland perennial in the saxifrage family (Saxifragaceae) that is closely related to heucheras and tiarellas and shares their preference for partial to full woodland shade — it grows as a clump of basal, roundly heart-shaped, hairy, dark-green leaves (remarkably similar to Heuchera leaves) and in late spring produces tall, slender, upright flower stems to 24 inches, each bearing an elegant, elongated spike of small, nodding, greenish-white to cream, urn-shaped flowers with the petals elaborately fringed (cut into narrow, thread-like segments) at their margins — creating a delicate, lacey floral effect along the entire length of the spike. The flowers fade through white to pink to rose-red as they age, so a single spike carries multiple color stages simultaneously. The foliage rosettes are attractive year-round and are semi-evergreen in mild winters. Self-seeds freely in moist shade. A graceful, refined, and underused shade perennial for zone 5–9 woodland gardens.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring before shoots emerge, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; yellow fairy bells establishes steadily and expands into an increasingly attractive clump — the rhizome is somewhat sensitive to drying out, so divisions should be replanted promptly and kept moist during establishment.

directions: Divide rhizomes or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; yellow fairy bells is a Korean woodland perennial in the lily family (Liliaceae / Colchicaceae) that shares the same arching, leafy-stemmed, graceful habit as the related Solomon's seal (Polygonatum) and false Solomon's seal (Maianthemum) — each stem is an elegant, arching or erect, 24–36-inch wand clothed in broadly oval, parallel-veined, bright-green, sessile leaves from whose tips in spring hang clusters of pendant, narrowly cylindrical to tubular, rich golden-yellow, bell-shaped flowers with slightly flared mouths, creating a warm, jewel-like, yellow pendant-bell display at the shoot tips that brightens the spring shade garden. The yellow color is more vivid and saturated than most shade perennials, which tend to flowers in white, lavender, or pink, making D. flavens a valuable source of true yellow in the shade garden. Black berries follow the flowers. The rhizomes spread slowly to form a gradually expanding clump. An increasingly popular and valued shade perennial for the woodland and shade garden in zone 5–8.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring as new growth begins, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; yellow toad lily requires consistently moist, humus-rich soil and mulching to maintain the cool, moist root zone it prefers — avoid dry, exposed, or hot positions where the shallow rhizomes will suffer drought stress.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; yellow toad lily is a Japanese woodland perennial toad lily of exceptional and rare distinction — while the vast majority of toad lily species (Tricyrtis) produce white or pale-lavender flowers spotted with purple or maroon (the pattern that gives them their toad-spotted common name), T. macranthopsis produces large, clear, unspotted, bright-golden-yellow to warm-yellow, nodding, six-petaled, bell-shaped flowers hanging from the leaf axils along the arching stems in late summer and fall, with just light maroon spotting in the throat. The clear yellow, large, pendant-bell flower form is unique in the genus and exceptionally beautiful in the partial-shade garden where yellow is a relatively scarce flower color. The arching, leafy stems carry broadly oval, clasping, bright-green leaves similar to other toad lilies. It blooms in late summer and fall, extending toad lily color later in the season. A treasured, somewhat difficult-to-source but readily grown toad lily for shade gardens in zone 4–9.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, or plant container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; balm-leaved archangel is considerably clumping and non-invasive compared to the spreading deadnettle (L. purpureum) and yellow archangel (L. galeobdolon) — it stays in a well-behaved, gradually expanding clump and is not a runner or aggressive spreader.

directions: Divide clumps or plant container specimens in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade; balm-leaved archangel is a Southern European woodland perennial dead-nettle (Lamiaceae) that is considerably more refined and better-behaved than its commonly grown relatives — it produces a clump-forming, upright, non-spreading, bushy habit (unlike the invasively spreading yellow archangel) with large, broadly oval, prominently veined, soft-hairy, dark-green, nettle-like leaves (resembling Melissa officinalis in both leaf form and texture) and in late spring produces whorls of large, two-lipped, rich deep-pink to rosy-magenta flowers considerably larger and more showy than the typical small-flowered deadnettles. The combination of large, attractive, deep-rose flowers in a shade-tolerant, non-invasive, clumping perennial is unusual and valuable. It dies back after summer dormancy in hot climates but regrows in fall. Goes dormant in summer heat. Semi-evergreen in mild winters. Excellent as a refined spring-blooming shade perennial for zone 4–9 woodland gardens where non-invasive groundcovers are needed.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
